question: [File: 'File Name: rest/service/entityService.js -> Scope: High: Any impacts caused by Tampering with submitted transactions'] [Function: getEncodedId()] Can an unprivileged external user submit a crafted entityIdString that passes EntityId.isValidEntityId() but resolves to a different account's encoded ID than intended, causing a transaction to be routed to the wrong entity and resulting in unauthorized fund movement or state change?

[File: 'File Name: rest/service/entityService.js -> Scope: High: Any impacts caused by Tampering with submitted transactions'] [Function: getEncodedId()] Can an unprivileged external user supply an entityIdString that satisfies both EntityId.isValidEntityId() and AccountAlias.isValid() simultaneously, causing ambiguous branch selection in the if/else-if chain and resolving to an unintended entity ID, thereby tampering with which account a submitted transaction targets?

[File: 'File Name: rest/service/entityService.js -> Scope: High: Any impacts caused by Tampering with submitted transactions'] [Function: getEncodedId()] Can an unprivileged external user pass requireResult=false to getEncodedId() and receive a null return value that a calling transaction handler treats as a valid zero-ID, causing the transaction to be applied against entity 0 or an unintended default entity?

[File: 'File Name: rest/service/entityService.js -> Scope: High: Any impacts caused by Tampering with submitted transactions'] [Function: getEntityIdFromEvmAddress()] Can an unprivileged external user craft an EVM address whose hex encoding, after Buffer.from(entityId.evmAddress, 'hex'), matches a different entity's stored evm_address in the database, causing getEntityIdFromEvmAddress() to return the wrong entity ID and redirecting a submitted transaction to an unintended account?

[File: 'File Name: rest/service/entityService.js -> Scope: High: Any impacts caused by Tampering with submitted transactions'] [Function: getEntityIdFromEvmAddress()] Can an unprivileged external user submit an EVM address where entityFromEvmAddressQuery uses 'deleted <> true' without COALESCE, causing entities with a NULL deleted column to be excluded from lookup, and thereby making a valid live entity appear non-existent so that a transaction targeting it is rejected or misdirected?

[File: 'File Name: rest/service/entityService.js -> Scope: High: Any impacts caused by Tampering with submitted transactions'] [Function: getAccountFromAlias()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the inconsistency between entityFromAliasQuery using 'coalesce(deleted, false) <> true' and entityFromEvmAddressQuery using 'deleted <> true' (no COALESCE) to make the same underlying entity appear deleted via one lookup path but alive via another, causing split-brain transaction routing?

[File: 'File Name: rest/service/entityService.js -> Scope: High: Any impacts caused by Tampering with submitted transactions'] [Function: getAccountFromAlias()] Can an unprivileged external user submit a transaction with an alias that matches multiple alive entities in the database, triggering the 'Multiple alive entities matching alias' error and causing a denial-of-service where all transactions referencing that alias are permanently rejected?

[File: 'File Name: rest/service/entityService.js -> Scope: High: Any impacts caused by Tampering with submitted transactions'] [Function: getEntityIdFromEvmAddress()] Can an unprivileged external user submit a transaction referencing an EVM address that matches multiple alive entities (rows.length > 1), triggering the multipleEvmAddressMatch error and causing a denial-of-service that permanently blocks all transactions targeting that EVM address?

[File: 'File Name: rest/service/entityService.js -> Scope: High: Any impacts caused by Tampering with submitted transactions'] [Function: getAccountIdFromAlias()] Can an unprivileged external user submit a transaction with an alias that resolves to null (no matching entity) while requireResult defaults to true, causing a NotFoundError that is surfaced to the caller in a way that leaks internal entity existence information, enabling enumeration of valid accounts to target in future tampered transactions?

[File: 'File Name: rest/service/entityService.js -> Scope: High: Any impacts caused by Tampering with submitted transactions'] [Function: getEncodedId()] Can an unprivileged external user supply a negative encoded entity ID string (matching encodedEntityIdRegex /^-?\\d{1,19}$/) that passes EntityId.isValidEntityId() and is then parsed via parseFromEncodedId() to produce a valid BigInt within [minEncodedId, maxEncodedId], resolving to an entity the attacker does not own and tampering with the transaction target?

[File: 'File Name: rest/service/entityService.js -> Scope: High: Any impacts caused by Tampering with submitted transactions'] [Function: getEncodedId()] Can an unprivileged external user craft an entityIdString in the 'realm.num' two-part format that, after systemShard is prepended by parseFromString(), resolves to a different entity than the 'shard.realm.num' three-part form of the same address, causing transaction routing inconsistency?

[File: 'File Name: rest/service/entityService.js -> Scope: High: Any impacts caused by Tampering with submitted transactions'] [Function: getEntityIdFromEvmAddress()] Can an unprivileged external user submit a transaction with an EVM address that has a valid 40-hex-char format but whose Buffer.from(..., 'hex') conversion silently truncates or pads bytes due to odd-length input, causing the query to match a different entity's stored binary evm_address and redirecting the transaction?

[File: 'File Name: rest/service/entityService.js -> Scope: High: Any impacts caused by Tampering with submitted transactions'] [Function: getEncodedId()] Can an unprivileged external user submit an EVM address with a '0x' prefix that is stripped by stripHexPrefix() before being passed to getEntityIdFromEvmAddress(), and then craft a second address without the prefix that produces a different Buffer.from() result, causing two syntactically different inputs to resolve to the same entity and enabling transaction replay or confusion?

[File: 'File Name: rest/service/entityService.js -> Scope: High: Any impacts caused by Tampering with submitted transactions'] [Function: getAccountFromAlias()] Can an unprivileged external user submit a base32 alias string that passes accountAliasRegex /^(\\d{1,5}\\.){0,2}[A-Z2-7]+$/ but whose base32.decode() output collides with the stored binary alias of a different account, causing getAccountFromAlias() to return the wrong entity and tampering with the transaction's target account?

[File: 'File Name: rest/service/entityService.js -> Scope: High: Any impacts caused by Tampering with submitted transactions'] [Function: getAccountFromAlias()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that AccountAlias.fromString() calls base32.decode() which 'allows lower case letters, padding, and auto corrects some typos' to submit a malformed alias that is normalized to match a victim's alias, causing the transaction to be attributed to the victim's account?

[File: 'File Name: rest/service/entityService.js -> Scope: High: Any impacts caused by Tampering with submitted transactions'] [Function: getEncodedId()] Can an unprivileged external user submit an entityIdString that is a long-form EVM address (first 12 bytes all zeros) whose num portion exactly equals maxNum (2^38-1), causing parseFromEvmAddress() to classify it as an account num alias rather than an opaque EVM address, and thereby resolving to a different entity than intended?

[File: 'File Name: rest/service/entityService.js -> Scope: High: Any impacts caused by Tampering with submitted transactions'] [Function: getEncodedId()] Can an unprivileged external user submit an EVM address where the first 24 hex chars are all zeros (longFormEvmAddressPrefix) but the num portion exceeds maxNum, causing it to be treated as an opaque EVM address and routed through getEntityIdFromEvmAddress() instead of getEncodedId()'s direct path, potentially resolving to a different entity?

[File: 'File Name: rest/service/entityService.js -> Scope: High: Any impacts caused by Tampering with submitted transactions'] [Function: isValidAccount()] Can an unprivileged external user submit a transaction with an accountId that passes isValidAccount() because entityExistenceQuery has no 'deleted <> true' filter, meaning deleted entities are still considered valid, allowing transactions to be submitted against deleted accounts and causing unexpected state changes?

[File: 'File Name: rest/service/entityService.js -> Scope: High: Any impacts caused by Tampering with submitted transactions'] [Function: isValidAccount()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that entityExistenceQuery only checks entity.TYPE (not deleted status) to submit transactions targeting a deleted entity that still exists in the entity table, bypassing the deletion guard and causing the transaction to be processed against a logically removed account?

[File: 'File Name: rest/service/entityService.js -> Scope: High: Any impacts caused by Tampering with submitted transactions'] [Function: getEncodedId()] Can an unprivileged external user submit an entityIdString that throws an InvalidArgumentError inside the try/catch block, causing the catch to re-throw InvalidArgumentError.forParams(paramName) with a different error message than the original, leaking information about which validation step failed and enabling targeted crafting of tampered transaction inputs?

[File: 'File Name: rest/service/entityService.js -> Scope: High: Any impacts caused by Tampering with submitted transactions'] [Function: getEncodedId()] Can an unprivileged external user submit an entityIdString that is neither a valid EntityId nor a valid AccountAlias, causing getEncodedId() to fall through to the final 'throw InvalidArgumentError.forParams(paramName)' without entering the try/catch, and exploit the difference in error handling paths to fingerprint the validation logic for crafting future tampered inputs?

[File: 'File Name: rest/service/entityService.js -> Scope: High: Any impacts caused by Tampering with submitted transactions'] [Function: getAccountFromAlias()] Can an unprivileged external user submit a transaction with an alias that resolves to an entity whose 'deleted' column is NULL (not false), and exploit the 'coalesce(deleted, false) <> true' condition in entityFromAliasQuery to include that entity in results, while the same entity would be excluded by entityFromEvmAddressQuery's 'deleted <> true', causing inconsistent transaction routing depending on lookup method?

[File: 'File Name: rest/service/entityService.js -> Scope: High: Any impacts caused by Tampering with submitted transactions'] [Function: getEntityIdFromEvmAddress()] Can an unprivileged external user submit a transaction with an EVM address targeting an entity whose 'deleted' column is NULL, which is excluded by 'deleted <> true' (since NULL <> true is NULL/false in SQL), causing the entity to appear non-existent and the transaction to be rejected or rerouted, effectively denying service for that entity?

[File: 'File Name: rest/service/entityService.js -> Scope: High: Any impacts caused by Tampering with submitted transactions'] [Function: getEncodedId()] Can an unprivileged external user submit a shard.realm.evmAddress format string where the shard and realm differ from systemShard/systemRealm, causing parseFromString() to throw an error that is caught and re-thrown as InvalidArgumentError.forParams(paramName), and use this behavior to probe which shard/realm combinations are configured, enabling targeted transaction tampering?

[File: 'File Name: rest/service/entityService.js -> Scope: High: Any impacts caused by Tampering with submitted transactions'] [Function: getEncodedId()] Can an unprivileged external user submit a crafted encoded entity ID at the boundary of maxEncodedId (2^63-1) or minEncodedId (-2^63) that, after BigInt arithmetic in parseFromEncodedId(), produces shard/realm/num values that map to a system entity (e.g., treasury account 0.0.2 or fee collection account 0.0.802), causing a submitted transaction to be tampered to target a system account?
